sourceRef.citation: Michelle Seng Ah Lee et al. 2026

statement.en: Fairness under uncertainty in sequential decisions
statement.summary: Fair machine learning (ML) methods help identify and mitigate the risk that algorithms encode or automate social injustices. Algorithmic approaches alone cannot resolve structural inequalities, but they can support socio-technical decision systems by surfacing discriminatory biases, clarifying trade-offs, and enabling governance. Although fairness is well studied in supervised learning, many real ML applications are online and sequential, with prior decisions informing future ones. Each decision is taken under uncertainty due to unobserved counterfactuals and finite samples, with dire consequences for under-represented groups, systematically under-observed due to historical exclusion and selective feedback. A bank cannot know whether a denied loan would have been repaid, and may have less

proposer: Michelle Seng Ah Lee, Kirtan Padh, David Watson et al.

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The axiom addresses an active research frontier (fairness in sequential decision-making under uncertainty). Core claims are partially validated empirically but lack complete theoretical closure. The problem formulation is sound, but solutions remain provisional and context-dependent, with ongoing validation in preprint/conference stage (2026 date indicates near-future publication cycle).
